title: Physical Quantities & SI Units
subtitle: The 7 SI base units and physical quantities explained for Class 11 Physics (NCERT)

blocks.heading: The Idea
blocks.body:
To measure something is to compare it with an agreed standard called a unit. Saying "the rope is 5" is meaningless; "5 metres" is not.
The world agrees on one system — the SI (Système International). It has just seven base units, and every other unit (for speed, force, energy…) is a derived unit built by combining them.

blocks.heading: The Formula
blocks.body:
A few SI base quantities and their units:
length → metre (m)
mass → kilogram (kg)
time → second (s)
Derived units combine base units, for example:
Symbols: m = metre, kg = kilogram, s = second, N = newton (the unit of force).

blocks.heading: Real-World Example
blocks.body: A carpenter in Bengaluru and a steel mill in Germany can fit parts together only because both use the same metre and the same kilogram. GPS, online trade, medicine doses and space missions all depend on everyone sharing one set of standards.

blocks.heading: Try It At Home
blocks.body: Measure your height with a piece of thread, then lay the thread against a ruler marked in centimetres and convert to metres. Notice you needed the ruler — a standard — before the number meant anything.

blocks.heading: What If…
blocks.body: Long ago a "foot" was literally the length of the local king's foot, so it changed from place to place. How would science or trade work if your metre and mine were different lengths?

quiz.question: Which of these is NOT an SI base unit?
quiz.explanation: The newton is a derived unit (1 N = 1 kg·m·s⁻²). The seven base units are the metre, kilogram, second, ampere, kelvin, mole and candela.
